speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three of the interview with Jacob Rosenberg. Jacob, you were preparing to read a poem to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The poem is related to the difficulties of language. And here I'm trying to explain my feeling about the difficulties I experienced. And the poem I call, 'No Exit.'

speaker: interviewer
text: 'How do you describe it? What alphabet do you employ? What words, what language, what silence, what scream? I consulted all the linguists, all the poets, all the wise men, men of knowledge, and then God himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: He dropped his head in silence. And with a sigh, he pointed to his darkest labyrinth, his blackest labyrinth he did not dare to enter. It had no exit.' [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: If we can return now to the ghetto, can you tell us about the authority structures that existed in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans were masters forever of organizing, of order. It was inherent in their personality. It didn't took them long to find the right people to work for them. I believe that when the ghetto was established, there was about 31 people in charge of-- of communal life. Within about a day or two or three, they eliminated 30. And they left only one, and this was Rumkowski. Obviously they had a psychologist. And they understood that he is the material for them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he became, so to speak, the dictator. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's too-- the time is too-- too near to us, the tragedy is too-- it's too-- almost still living, it's still warm. But in years to come, I think there will-- there will emerge a man like-- perhaps a-- a great authority, a literary capacity, who be able to tell this funny little fellow who-- who dreamt about becoming a king of the Jews. And by-- and for that sort of vision and dream, he sold out his brethren to the devil. This does not mean if he wouldn't do it, someone else wouldn't. But this does not exonerate him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I think what-- what I feel, in particular in this situation, that a man who is condemned to die in this-- he has the right to choose his death. And what he did, he deprived us of the choice. Because if we knew what's going on, I'm sure that the people would stand-- stand up against the Germans with-- with their fist, with-- with-- with nothing. With-- with-- they would stand up. Of course, they would-- we would be slaughtered. But he-- he took away this right from us. And because Germany, not the Nazis, but Germany, was interested to have this murder done orderly, they needed a man like him, who will keep the secret, who will prepare transport after transport to be sent away to be exterminated.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he prepare the transports?

speaker: interviewee
text: They conducted lists, and they had a more-or-less idea, who is still-- can be used for work. And he-- he constantly kept us-- he told us constantly that we're going out to work. But how can a sane person [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] believe that a three-year-old child is sent out to work? I think it-- I think you've got to be really sort of-- I don't know. Either this is such-- it such a cynicism. How can one believe that a man who got two days to live that he's going to-- he's going to-- he's taking out to-- to work? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was obvious. Yet, he-- he kept denying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when a-- when a human being is about to die, and you leave him the slightest gap of hope, he'll-- he'll believe you. And that's what the Germans did. Before entering the gas chambers, they gave you a piece of soap and a towel, so you can dry yourself. Now look, if-- if you give someone a piece of soap, he surely is not going to-- you're not going to die. What for does he need the soap? Do you recall any searches taking place when you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Yeah. I remember-- like all searches, they came in. They were looking for-- for arms. They were looking for weapons. We had no weapons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any times that you hid?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any times that you hid?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- there were weapons, but very few, you know, that's not-- in Lodz, there was very few weapons. We had no weapons. We had no supplies. We had no means-- means of producing weapons. So-- there were-- there were means, of course there were. But we were watched constantly by-- by-- by the authorities, and the-- their henchmen, you know, those who they appointed to watch us. No, we had no weapons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness people being punished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course we witnessed people being punished, hung and beaten. Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about new arrivals? Did new people come into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. People came from Western Europe, from Czechoslovakia, from Germany, from Austria. People constantly came. They could not understand what's going on. They were not prepared for that sort of-- of life. And they soon died out. And then they were sent out from ghetto. Some of them were immediately sent out from ghetto. And Rumkowski was also afraid of-- of competition amongst them. Because amongst those Jews, were eloquent well-speaking German Jews, intelligent, who had most probably a better understanding and communication with the German authorities than he ever had. But what he had, they didn't have was probably a sense of-- of dictatorial attitudes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of Jewish communal life existed in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a-- mainly a cultural form of communal life, and also a self-help. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Me, as a part of-- of-- of a movement, we were underground movement, Bundist movement. We-- we kept-- we kept alive a-- a cultural [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] form. We still made sure that we read books. And we talked about the political structure of the world, and we had always lecturers and-- to keep alive, to keep alive the fires of hope, and be prepared for another-- for after, to be prepared for a new tomorrow. That's-- yeah. I remember we-- we celebrated the memory of Peretz and Sholem Aleichem, of most of Yiddish classic writers, and of-- of-- also I remember that we-- we commemorated the murder of Henryk Ehrlich and Victor Alter. We knew about it, the two major leaders of-- of our movement, murdered by Stalin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there political meetings taking place inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, but of course, [INAUDIBLE], you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any time when people demonstrated against Rumkowski?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In the initial stages, but he called out the German police, and they disbursed the demonstration. It was the hunger was so desperate. People became so desperate with hunger that they had nothing to lose. And they-- they went out to-- to-- to demonstrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was there. I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any illnesses in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Tuberculosis was-- was rampant. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oscillation of bones, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] typhus. You name it, everything was there. A lot people, you know, were together when the ghetto was closed. I think there was about-- 100-- 179,000 Jews. And I would say that about, within about three or four years, 20% died, or more. I have-- I have no statistics. I don't know. But death was part of-- of-- of living.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your health during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not bad. I was young, and a-- a strong young boy. We were-- we were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't say that we-- we were all healthy in that sense. But we kept-- we kept-- we kept ourselves. We-- we did the best we could to hygienically and-- and-- whatever food we had, we accommodated ourselves to eat less. My father was not well. He suffered from bone oscillation, very painful. But otherwise, we were not sick at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of hygiene or sanitary conditions did you establish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we had plenty-- water was there. So we washed in cold water. And we had to. This was-- but if you-- if you are attuned like this to look after your hygienically, you find ways. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was-- it was a struggle. Everything was a struggle. Nothing came easy in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was taking place outside of the ghettos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Initially we didn't know. For the first year or two, we didn't know. But then we became aware. My mother worked in the-- in the factory-- cleaning factory. And they took out-- and I-- I can't remember the date exactly now. But one of the resettlement, they took to Chelmno. Chelmno, it was a-- an extermination camp. And they brought back the things together. Imagine how obvious this was. And my mother found clothing from my friend, from my school friend, and with a bullet, a bullet through. And-- his clothes were full of blood, was bullets-ridden. The clothing was bullet-- bullets-ridden. And we knew about it that this-- this was taking place. They had, I think, 55,000 Jews they took out of the ghetto then. Or was it 55-- I think, I can't remember the number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that happen regularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course, of course. From time to time, they felt-- you see there was this competition between the ghetto authorities [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the political authorities of Germany, like the SS. They don't want-- they want the Jews to be murdered, and the economic authorities wanted the Jews-- to retain the-- the-- their Jewish labor force, their cheap labor force. For them it was a tremendous source of income. But they didn't have any-- the SS didn't have an income. So as they said, what do we need the Jews for? We don't want them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall any-- any times when a lot of people were deported at-- at one time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I told you. In 1940-- in 1940-- no. In 1941 there was a great deportation. And then it was continuous, 10,000, 5,000, you know. They emptied the ghetto orderly, like I said before. Once the SS start pressing on the-- on the authorities who run the ghetto. That guy is going to give you 5,000-- he'll give you 10,000 Jews. He'll give you 15,000 little kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any gypsies in the ghettos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I didn't know them personally, but I-- I-- I been next to their camp. There were about 5,000 they brought in. And I believe there was-- amongst them was not only gypsies. There were a lot of-- of Romanian people, perhaps academics, intellectuals. Because I remember that a friend of mine who worked cleaning up the place, found a woman. She was completely-- she was murdered.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was completely nude, lying on a bed with a pair of gloves. Now, no gypsy wore gloves. She had a pair of white gloves on her hand. So who knows who was there? Why was the gypsy camp kept separate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe this is a game, the-- the authorities who were interested in-- in-- in the economical gain from the ghetto. Because the gypsies were not productive-- not productive people. They couldn't work behind machines. So they were not tool makers. They were not carpenters. They were not tailors. So they kept them separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you believe sustained you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you believe sustained you? What-- what kept you alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hope. We dwelt-- we-- we-- again here, I got-- I got something which I may read, and tell you what-- how I see it. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] Now, I go here to after-- this is the liquidation of the ghetto. 'Two more long years went by. Years of threat and pain, from hunger blossomed death like grass after rain. And yet, [? we ?] [? live ?] [? with ?] hope, nourished by a delirious dream of a heroic messiah who speeds from the east to redeem, and the ghetto buzzed with rumors spread by starving Jews, figments of imagination. Dazzling felicitous news, news of gigantic battles, deliverer is coming, is here. Oh, how sweet the smell of this lie, how bitter the truth to fear.' That's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you write poetry when you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I began-- I actually wrote some poetry in camp. And funny enough, there was no paper. I got hold of a little bit of a-- a pencil. And I used to write on my hand, and read it to myself, I tried to memorize it. I did some of it, I did. And-- and I-- I wrote it after the war. But it was so-- it was not-- it was not-- it was not the thing I wanted to-- what I wanted-- It was the thing I wanted to say. But the form was not-- was only sort of limping behind the idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your niece born in the ghetto, your second niece?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was born in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in 1940-- 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- what about at the times when children were taken from the ghetto, what happened with your two nieces?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now here I'm-- I'm telling you. 'Then came summer. A dark date to remember, the year 1942. It's the 5th of September. That morning we were greeted by white placards with black news. Mothers, Germany demands your children, all sick and elderly Jews, they are to be resettled in another place. The Germans has sworn to it by their holy grace. Do not obstruct this, mothers. Bring your children freely. Let's not provoke a bath-- a bloodbath, and cause no more killing, so spoke the appointed agents. Their sinister pleading lie, mothers, give up your children and no one needs to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: The action began in the morning. With the break of dawn and by the blood red sunset, thousands of children were gone. And fathers stood there defeated, stood with dusky eyes, talked of ending it all. But maybe, wait a few days. Sure this German is cruel, mean, and wild. But is there a man in this world who will murder an innocent child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prior to this monstrous crime, my mother summoned her strength, anything but not my little children. I'd rather be burned. She made Pola run to the cemetery, and Ida to the windowless hall, and she stood alone in the street to face Germany's call.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was also in hiding. And I from the rubbish of the yard, saw Reuben from Kalisz cut down by a bullet from the guard. I heard him call out, oh god, what has this German done? But all was-- but all was frozen silence beneath the the mocking sun.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother stood nearby, petrified to breathe, one eye on the windowless hall, the other on the murderous deed. I'm not a believer in miracles. Yet one miracle did occur. When I opened the door for my home, and still I find my family there. But all beyond recognition, broken, fractured, and spent. My mother with night on her face, her back bent. And father moans in the dark, it's all in vain, in vain. The night is long and out there waits the train. But I'll never forget the children, their hushed-up cries, their grown-up silence, the terror in their eyes.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So, my mother's initiative saved those children for another two years of life. Do you have a poem about that also in Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have some poem in Yiddish, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to read it in Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know which book it is. I think it's-- no. I think this one here? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Perhaps, I read something else in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which is-- which is related to-- to-- to this sort of aspect of-- of-- of resettlement. This is my-- this is a poem. This is a poem, which talks about resettlement, and the-- the last day when we were taken away. [BACKROUND NOISE] This poem is called [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. Now, this tells you how this was not to lose the human faith, even in the face of this greatest ultimate tragedy, Jews did not lose their human faith. The family bond was so strong and was so-- it was something which I cannot understand how this came that we did not despairs. And we-- we kept together. This also helped the Germans to annihilate us. We were all so conscious of one another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it helped to sustain you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It helped in a sense to the last moment. It helped us to sustain us. But perhaps not only me, it helped all those who lived through the war to pick up where they left off, as far as human beings are concerned, as far as communal responsibility, as far-- as far as being a member of the human race.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there burials taking place inside the ghetto as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. But-- you know, without-- without ceremony, without anything, but just people were thrown into a ditch, a grave, gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no time to cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of people who were recording, collecting archives, or taking photographs?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was some. I-- I don't know personal of any. But there were people who-- who did it.
